sections.title: 0.1 The German Alphabet (Das deutsche Alphabet)
sections.intro: The German alphabet has **26 standard letters** plus four special characters: **ä, ö, ü** (umlauts) and **ß** (Eszett / scharfes S). Knowing the names of letters in German is essential for spelling your name, address, or email out loud (**buchstabieren**). Each vowel can appear with or without an umlaut, which changes both pronunciation and meaning (e.g., **schon** *already* vs **schön** *beautiful*).
tables: aspirated at start; silent after vowel
guttural at start; vowel-like at end
examples: **Wie schreibt man das?** = How do you spell that?
**Können Sie das buchstabieren?** = Can you spell that?
**Mein Name ist Müller: M-Ü-L-L-E-R.**

sections.title: 0.2 Umlauts and the Eszett (ä, ö, ü, ß)
sections.intro: German has **four extra letters** that English does not: **ä, ö, ü** (umlauts) and **ß** (Eszett, also called *scharfes S* — sharp S). Umlauts represent distinct vowel sounds and can be written as **ae, oe, ue** when special characters are unavailable (e.g., on an English keyboard). The **ß** is only used after long vowels and diphthongs and is replaced by **ss** in Switzerland and after short vowels (e.g., **Straße** but **muss**). Capital ß (**ẞ**) exists since 2017 but is rare.
examples: **Müller** can be written **Mueller** on an international keyboard.
**Straße** = **Strasse** in Switzerland.
Umlauts change meaning: **schon** (already) ≠ **schön** (beautiful); **Mutter** (mother) ≠ **Mütter** (mothers).

sections.title: 0.3 Spelling and Pronunciation Rules
sections.intro: Several letter combinations in German have predictable, fixed pronunciations. Mastering these makes both reading aloud and listening much easier. Note that **ch** has two sounds (soft after i/e/ä/ö/ü/consonants, hard after a/o/u/au), and **sp** / **st** at the start of a word are pronounced **shp** / **sht**.
tables: **ch** (after i, e, ä, ö, ü, consonants)
examples: **ich liebe dich** — soft *ch*, soft *ch*.
**Buch** vs **Bücher** — hard *ch* becomes soft *ch* with umlaut.
**Wein** sounds like English *vine*, **Wasser** like *vahsser*.

sections.title: 0.4 Capitalisation Rules
sections.intro: German capitalises **all nouns**, not just proper nouns. This is one of the most distinctive features of German orthography. Pronouns are lowercase except **Sie** (formal *you*) and **Ihr/Ihnen** (formal *your/to you*). The pronoun **ich** (*I*) is **never** capitalised in the middle of a sentence — unlike English.
examples: **Ich** lese ein **Buch** über die **deutsche Sprache**.
**Können Sie** mir helfen? (formal Sie capitalised)
Heute ist **Dienstag**, der erste **Mai**.

sections.title: 1.1 Gender (Geschlecht) – Recognising the Three Classes
sections.intro: Every German noun has one of three grammatical genders: **masculine (der)**, **feminine (die)**, or **neuter (das)**. Unlike English, gender is a grammatical property of the word — not tied to biological sex. You must learn the article together with the noun. However, there are reliable patterns: nouns ending in **-ung, -heit, -keit, -schaft, -tion** are always feminine; nouns ending in **-er, -ling, -ismus** tend to be masculine; nouns ending in **-chen, -lein** are always neuter. When in doubt, look for these suffixes first before guessing.

sections.title: 1.2 Plural Formation (Pluralbildung)
sections.intro: German has nine plural patterns — far more than English. The most important strategy: **always learn the plural form when you learn a new noun** (e.g. der Hund, die Hunde). Key patterns to memorise: most masculine nouns add **-e** (der Tag → die Tage); most feminine nouns add **-n/-en** (die Frau → die Frauen); neuter nouns often add **-er** with umlaut (das Kind → die Kinder); nouns ending in **-er** often have no change (der Lehrer → die Lehrer). Foreign words often add **-s** (das Auto → die Autos).

examples.en: The child plays in the garden.
The children play in the garden.
The man works a lot.
The men work a lot.
The book is interesting.
The books are interesting.

sections.title: 1.3 The Four Cases (Die vier Fälle)
sections.intro: Cases tell us the grammatical role of a noun in a sentence. German has four cases. Instead of changing the noun itself (as some languages do), German changes the article and sometimes the adjective. Read this short paragraph and notice how the same noun (der Hund) changes form: **[N] ****Der Hund** schläft. [A] Ich streichle **den Hund**. [D] Ich gebe **dem Hund** Wasser. [G] Das Spielzeug **des H
tables: Subject – who/what is doing the action
**Der Hund** bellt laut.  (The dog barks loudly.)
Direct object – directly affected by the action
Ich sehe **den Hund**.  (I see the dog.)
Indirect object – to/for whom something is done
Ich gebe **dem Hund** Futter.  (I give the dog food.)
Das ist das Spielzeug **des Hundes**.  (That is the dog's toy.)

sections.title: 1.4 Compound Nouns (Zusammengesetzte Nomen)
sections.intro: German freely combines words to create new nouns. The compound can be as long as needed. The **last element** always determines the gender. Usually no article or linking sound is needed, but sometimes **-s-**, **-n-**, or **-en-** is inserted between components. Chapter 2

sections.title: 2.1 Definite Articles (Bestimmte Artikel) – "the"
sections.intro: The definite article (the) changes form depending on **gender**, **case**, and **number**. This is called declension. The four cases are: **Nominative** (subject doing the action), **Accusative** (direct object), **Dative** (indirect object), **Genitive** (possession). The key shortcut: Nominative and Accusative are often the same — they only differ for masculine nouns (der → den). Feminine and neuter are identical in Nom/Acc. Focus first on Nominative vs. Dative as these are most frequent in everyday speech.

sections.title: 2.2 Indefinite Articles (Unbestimmte Artikel) – "a / an"
sections.intro: The indefinite article (a/an) follows the same case system as the definite article, but there is **no plural form** — instead, German uses no article at all (or kein-). The pattern mirrors the definite article endings but starts from ein-. Tip: the Accusative masculine is the only change from Nominative — **ein → einen**. For feminine, neuter, and plural, the endings follow the same logic as der/die/das.

examples.en: That is a dog. (m, Nominative)
I have a brother. (m, Accusative)
He gives a woman flowers. (f, Dative)
A friend's car is broken. (m, Genitive)

sections.title: 2.3 Negative Article (Negativartikel) – kein
sections.intro: **kein** negates nouns. It follows the same endings as ein, but it also has plural forms (unlike ein). Ich habe **kein** Auto.  →  I have no car. (neuter) Das ist **keine** gute Idee.  →  That is not a good idea. (feminine) Er hat **keine** Geschwister.  →  He has no siblings. (plural) Ich komme **nicht** morgen.  →  I am not coming tomorrow. (negates the whole sentence)
tables: Negating a noun with an article
Ich habe **keinen** Hund. (I have no dog.)
Er hat **keine** Zeit. (He has no time.)
Negating a verb, adjective, or adverb
Ich nehme **nicht** das rote Buch. (I'm not taking the red book.)

examples.en: I have no car. (neuter)
That is not a good idea. (feminine)
He has no siblings. (plural)
I am not coming tomorrow. (negates the whole sentence)

sections.title: 2.4 Zero Article (Nullartikel)
sections.intro: In certain fixed contexts, German uses no article at all. Chapter 3

sections.title: 3.1 Personal Pronouns (Personalpronomen)
sections.intro: Personal pronouns also change with case. The Nominative forms (ich, du, er, sie, es, wir, ihr, sie, Sie) are used when the pronoun is the subject. The Accusative (mich, dich, ihn, sie, es, uns, euch, sie, Sie) is used for direct objects. The Dative (mir, dir, ihm, ihr, ihm, uns, euch, ihnen, Ihnen) is used for indirect objects. **Memory tip:** the Accusative only differs from Nominative for the third person singular masculine: **er → ihn**.

examples.en: He sees me. (Accusative)
She gives me the book. (Dative)
We meet him at 5. (Accusative)
I help her. (Dative)
He writes us a message. (Dative)

sections.title: 3.2 Possessive Pronouns (Possessivpronomen)
sections.intro: Possessive pronouns (mein, dein, sein, ihr...) must agree with the **gender of the noun they describe**, not the owner. So a man says **meine Mutter** (my mother — feminine noun) and a woman also says **mein Vater** (my father — masculine noun). The endings follow the kein- declension pattern. The hardest part: **sein** means both 'his' and 'its'; **ihr** means both 'her' and 'their' — context resolves the ambiguity.

examples.en: That is my brother. (m, Nominative)
I see your dog. (m, Accusative)
We give our mother flowers. (f, Dative)
That is his car. / That is its car.
What is your name? (formal)

sections.title: 3.3 Reflexive Pronouns (Reflexivpronomen)
sections.intro: Reflexive pronouns refer back to the subject of the sentence. They are used with reflexive verbs, which are very common in German. In accusative and dative, most pronouns are the same as personal pronouns, except **sich** (3rd person singular & plural, and formal Sie). Ich **wasche mich**.  →  I wash myself. (the whole body) Du **rasierst dich**.  →  You shave (yourself). Er **freut sich** über da
tables: to be happy about / to look forward to
Wir freuen uns auf den Urlaub.
Ich fühle mich heute nicht gut.
Darf ich mich vorstellen? Ich bin Anna.

examples.en: I wash myself. (the whole body)
You shave (yourself).
He is pleased about the gift.
I wash my hands. (lit: I wash to-myself the hands)
He brushes his teeth.
She combs her hair.

sections.title: 3.4 Demonstrative Pronouns (Demonstrativpronomen)
sections.intro: Demonstrative pronouns point to specific things. The most important are **dieser** (this/these) and **jener** (that/those). They decline exactly like the definite article, with the same endings. **Dieser** Mann ist nett.  →  This man is nice. (m, Nominative) Ich mag **dieses** Buch.  →  I like this book. (n, Accusative) Wir geben **dieser** Frau den Brief.  →  We give this woman the letter. (f, Da

examples.en: I like this book. (n, Accusative)
We give this woman the letter. (f, Dative)

sections.title: 3.5 Interrogative Pronouns (Interrogativpronomen)
sections.intro: Interrogative pronouns are the question words you use to ask about people, things, and possession. The **wer**-group asks about *people* and changes form with the case: **wer** (who, Nominative), **wen** (whom, Accusative), **wem** (to whom, Dative), **wessen** (whose, Genitive). **Was** asks about *things* and **welcher / welche / welches** asks "which one?", declining like **dieser**. **Wer** kommt heute?  →  Who is coming today? **Was** kostet das?  →  What does that cost?
tables: **Wen** rufst du an? (Whom are you calling?)
**Wem** gehört das? (To whom does this belong?)
**Wessen** Buch ist das? (Whose book is this?)
**Welches** Auto ist deins? (Which car is yours?)

sections.title: 3.6 Indefinite Pronouns (Indefinitivpronomen)
sections.intro: Indefinite pronouns refer to people, things, or quantities that are *unspecified* — when you don't name exactly who or what you mean. Some stay the same in every position (**etwas** = something, **nichts** = nothing), while others decline like nouns or adjectives (**jemand / niemand**, **alle / einige / viele**). The pronoun **man** means "one / you / people in general" and always takes a 3rd-person singular verb. **Jemand** hat angerufen.  →  Someone called. **Man** spricht hier Deutsch.  →  People speak German here.
tables: Always singular, takes 3rd pers. sing. verb
Ich kenne **niemanden** hier. (I know no one here.)
Möchtest du **etwas** essen? (Do you want something to eat?)
**Einige** Leute kommen nicht. (Some people aren't coming.)
**Viele** Kinder spielen draußen. (Many children play outside.)
**Jeder** Mensch macht Fehler. (Every person makes mistakes.)

sections.title: 4.1 Predicative vs. Attributive Adjectives
sections.intro: German adjectives behave differently depending on their position. **Predicative adjectives** (after sein/werden/bleiben) have NO ending — Das Buch ist interessant. **Attributive adjectives** (before a noun) MUST carry a case ending. The ending chosen depends on what article (if any) precedes the adjective. There are three declension tables: weak (after der/die/das), mixed (after ein/kein), and strong (no article). Master the weak declension first as it is most regular.
tables: After sein, werden, bleiben, scheinen, wirken
Der Mann ist **groß**. Das Essen ist **lecker**. Sie wird **alt**.
Yes – depends on article + gender + case
der **große** Mann, das **leckere** Essen, die **alte** Frau

sections.title: 4.2 Weak Declension – after definite articles (der/die/das)
sections.intro: After a definite article, the adjective carries minimal information (since the article already shows gender/case). The endings are mostly **-en**, with -e in the nominative (all genders) and accusative (feminine and neuter). Das ist der **alte** Bahnhof.  →  That is the old station. (m, Nominative) Wir besuchen die **schöne** Stadt.  →  We visit the beautiful city. (f, Accusative) Er wohnt in dem

examples.en: That is the old station. (m, Nominative)
We visit the beautiful city. (f, Accusative)
He lives in the small house. (n, Dative)

examples.de: Die Bücher des **bekannten** Autors sind gut.
examples.en: The books of the famous author are good. (m, Genitive)

sections.title: 4.3 Mixed Declension – after indefinite articles / possessives
sections.intro: After an **indefinite article** (ein/eine/ein) or possessive (mein, dein...), the adjective must supply the missing gender signal in Nominative and Accusative. In cases where the article has **no ending** (ein-, not eine-), the adjective compensates by taking the strong ending from the definite article. In all other positions the ending is **-en**. Pattern to remember: wherever ein- has no ending, the adjective steps in with the full ending.

examples.en: I have an old dog. (m, Accusative)
That is my little sister. (f, Nominative)
We play with a small ball. (m, Dative)

sections.title: 4.4 Strong Declension – no article
sections.intro: When there is no article at all, the adjective must carry all the gender and case information itself. The endings mirror the definite article endings. **Guter** Wein schmeckt wunderbar.  →  Good wine tastes wonderful. (m, Nominative) Ich trinke **kaltes** Wasser.  →  I drink cold water. (n, Accusative) Mit **frischer** Luft schläft man gut.  →  One sleeps well with fresh air. (f, Dative)

examples.en: I drink cold water. (n, Accusative)
One sleeps well with fresh air. (f, Dative)

sections.title: 4.5 Comparison of Adjectives (Komparation)
sections.intro: Most adjectives form the comparative by adding **-er** and the superlative with **am ___sten**. Mein Auto ist **schnell**.  →  My car is fast. Dein Auto ist **schneller**.  →  Your car is faster. Sein Auto ist **am schnellsten**.  →  His car is the fastest. Peter ist **größer als** Maria.  →  Peter is taller than Maria. (als = than) Maria ist **genauso groß wie** Anna.  →  Maria is just as tall as

examples.en: My car is fast.
Your car is faster.
His car is the fastest.
Peter is taller than Maria. (als = than)
Maria is just as tall as Anna. (genauso…wie = as…as)

examples.de: Das ist das **beste** Restaurant in der Stadt.
examples.en: That is the best restaurant in the city.

sections.title: 5.1 Present Tense (Präsens) – Regular Verbs
sections.intro: Regular German verbs follow a predictable conjugation pattern. Remove the **-en** infinitive ending to get the stem, then add the personal endings: **-e, -st, -t, -en, -t, -en**. The present tense covers three meanings: ich mache = I make / I am making / I do make. **Watch the spelling rules:** stems ending in -t or -d add an extra -e- for pronunciation (du arbeitest, er wartet). Stems ending in -s/-ß/-z skip the -s- in du: du heißt (not heißst).

sections.title: 5.2 Stem-Changing Verbs (Starke Verben im Präsens)
sections.intro: Many of the most common German verbs change their **stem vowel** in the du and er/sie/es forms only. There are three main vowel changes: **a → ä** (fahren: du fährst), **e → i** (sprechen: du sprichst, er spricht), **e → ie** (lesen: du liest, er liest). These changes ONLY occur in 2nd and 3rd person singular — all other forms are regular. Learn these as vocabulary items: always note if a verb has a vowel change when you first encounter it.

sections.title: 5.3 The Essential Three: sein, haben, werden
sections.intro: **Sein** (to be) and **haben** (to have) are the two most important irregular verbs — they are also the auxiliary verbs used to form the perfect tense. Sein is highly irregular: ich bin, du bist, er ist, wir sind. Haben is more regular but has a key exception: du hast (not habst), er hat (not habt). Memorise these forms immediately — you will use them constantly in every tense and as helping verbs.

examples.en: I am a teacher.
We have a lot to do today.
It is getting dark.

examples.de: Er **ist** müde, aber er **hat** keine Zeit zu schlafen.
examples.en: He is tired, but he has no time to sleep.

sections.title: 5.4 Modal Verbs (Modalverben)
sections.intro: Modal verbs (können, müssen, dürfen, sollen, wollen, mögen/möchten) express ability, obligation, permission, intention, and desire. They follow a special pattern: the **ich** and **er/sie/es** forms are identical (no ending), and most modals have a vowel change in singular. Crucially, modals are always used with an **infinitive at the end** of the sentence: Ich kann Deutsch sprechen. Ich muss jetzt gehen. The infinitive moves to final position — this is a key German word order rule.
tables: Ich **kann** schwimmen. / Das **kann** sein. (That may be.)
Du **musst** jetzt schlafen. / Er **muss** krank sein.
Hier **darf** man nicht rauchen. / **Darf** ich fragen?
Du **sollst** pünktlich sein! / Er soll sehr nett sein.
Ich **will** Arzt werden. / Sie **wollen** morgen reisen.
Ich **möchte** einen Kaffee, bitte. / Wir **möchten** bezahlen.

examples.en: main verb infinitive at the END:
I can speak German well.
You must do the homework today.
We would like to reserve a room.
He is not allowed to park here.
She wants to travel to Japan next year.

sections.title: 5.5 Separable Verbs (Trennbare Verben)
sections.intro: The **Perfekt** (present perfect) is the main past tense in spoken German. It is formed with **haben or sein** + the **past participle** at the end. Most verbs use haben; motion and change-of-state verbs use sein (gehen → ist gegangen, kommen → ist gekommen). Regular past participles follow the pattern **ge- + stem + -t** (gemacht, gespielt). Irregular participles must be memorised (gegessen, geschlafen, gefahren). Separable verbs insert -ge- between the prefix and stem: aufgemacht.

sections.title: 5.6 Perfect Tense (Perfekt) – talking about the past
sections.intro: The **Präteritum** (simple past) is mainly used in **written German** and with the auxiliaries haben/sein and modals in speech. Regular verbs add **-te** endings (machte, spielte). Irregular verbs change their stem vowel (gehen → ging, kommen → kam, fahren → fuhr) — these must be memorised. The Präteritum of sein: war; of haben: hatte. In everyday conversation, use Perfekt for all other verbs and Präteritum only for sein/haben/modals.
tables: Most verbs; all transitive verbs (those that take an accusative object)
Ich **habe** ein Buch **gelesen**. / Er **hat** Fußball **gespielt**. / Wir **haben** Pizza **gegessen**.
Intransitive verbs of motion: gehen, fahren, kommen, laufen, reisen, fliegen, schwimmen…
Ich **bin** nach Hause **gegangen**. / Sie **ist** nach Berlin **gefahren**. / Wir **sind** geschwommen.
Verbs of state change: werden, wachsen, sterben, einschlafen, aufwachen…

examples.en: I have learned a lot today.
We found a good restaurant yesterday.
He got up at 7 o'clock.
Did you see the film?
She flew to Vienna last week.
I wrote him a message.

sections.title: 5.7 Simple Past (Präteritum)
sections.intro: The Präteritum is used mainly in written German (novels, newspapers). In spoken German it is used primarily for **sein, haben,** and **modal verbs**. Ich **war** gestern sehr müde.  →  I was very tired yesterday. Wir **hatten** keine Zeit.  →  We had no time. Er **konnte** nicht kommen, weil er krank **war**.  →  He couldn't come because he was sick. Als Kind **wollte** ich Astronaut werden.  →  A

examples.en: I was very tired yesterday.
We had no time.

examples.de: Er **konnte** nicht kommen, weil er krank **war**.
examples.en: He couldn't come because he was sick.
As a child I wanted to become an astronaut.

sections.title: 5.8 Imperative (Imperativ)
sections.intro: The imperative is used for commands, requests, and instructions. German has three imperative forms depending on who you are addressing. e → i / ie changing verbs use the changed vowel in the du imperative, but never add -e: sprechen: du sprichst → **Sprich** langsam! → Speak slowly! essen: du isst → **Iss** dein Gemüse! → Eat your vegetables! nehmen: du nimmst → **Nimm** das! → Take that! sehen: d

examples.en: Please come here!
Listen (for a moment)!
Do sit down!

sections.title: 5.9 Infinitiv ohne zu – Modal & Perception Verbs
sections.intro: Several verb groups are followed by a **bare infinitive** (no 'zu') at the end of the clause. Modal verbs (können, müssen, wollen, sollen, dürfen, mögen) always take a bare infinitive (see 5.4). Perception verbs (sehen, hören, fühlen, spüren) and **lassen** also take a bare infinitive when describing a directly perceived or caused action: lassen + Akkusativ + Infinitiv means either 'to have something done' (causative) or 'to let someone do something' (permission). The verbs bleiben and gehen can likewise be followed by a bare infinitive of posture/leisure verbs.
tables: I have to get up early.
lassen + Akk. + bare infinitive = 'have something done'
lassen + Akk. + bare infinitive = 'let someone do'
The child stays lying in bed.

examples.en: I hear you coming.
The hairdresser makes me wait a long time.
She remains standing at the phone.

sections.title: 6.2 Place Adverbs (Lokaladverbien)
sections.intro: German distinguishes between **location** (wo? / where?), **direction** (wohin? / where to?), and **origin** (woher? / where from?). Das Buch liegt **dort** auf dem Tisch.  →  The book is (lying) there on the table. (location) Komm bitte **hierher**!  →  Please come here! (direction toward speaker) Sie kommt **von draußen** herein.  →  She comes in from outside. (origin) Das Café ist **oben** im d

examples.de: Das Buch liegt **dort** auf dem Tisch.
examples.en: The book is (lying) there on the table. (location)
Please come here! (direction toward speaker)
She comes in from outside. (origin)

examples.de: Das Café ist **oben** im dritten Stock.
examples.en: The café is up on the third floor.

sections.title: 6.4 Degree Adverbs (Gradadverbien)
tables: Ich kann **kaum** glauben, was passiert ist.

sections.title: 6.5 Word Order with Adverbs – the TMP Rule
sections.intro: When a sentence contains multiple adverbials, the preferred order is: **Time → Manner → Place** Chapter 7

sections.title: 7.1 Accusative Prepositions
sections.intro: Accusative prepositions **always** trigger the Accusative case — no exceptions. The five main ones are: **durch** (through), **für** (for), **gegen** (against/around), **ohne** (without), **um** (around/at). Mnemonic: **D**uke **F**ido **G**oes **O**ver **U**nder (DFGOU). The key Accusative change to watch: masculine **der → den**, **ein → einen**, **kein → keinen**. Feminine, neuter, and plural forms are unchanged from Nominative.
tables: The gift is for my father.
The car drives around the block.

sections.title: 7.2 Dative Prepositions
sections.intro: Dative prepositions **always** trigger the Dative case. The nine core ones: **aus, bei, mit, nach, seit, von, zu, gegenüber, außer**. Mnemonic: **A**us **B**ei **M**it **N**ach **S**eit **V**on **Z**u (AB MNSV Z). The Dative changes: masculine/neuter **dem** (not der/das), feminine **der** (same spelling as masc. Nominative — context tells you), plural always adds **-n** to the noun if not already ending in -n. Contractions: von dem → vom, zu dem → zum, zu der → zur, bei dem → beim.
tables: Ich komme **aus der** Schweiz. / Das Haus ist **aus** Holz.
I come from Switzerland. / The house is made of wood.
Er wohnt **bei seiner** Mutter. / **Beim** Kochen höre ich Musik.
He lives with his mother. / While cooking I listen to music.
Ich fahre **mit dem** Zug. / Kommst du **mit mir**?
I travel by train. / Are you coming with me?
Ich fahre **nach** Berlin. / **Nach dem** Essen ruhe ich mich aus.
I am going to Berlin. / After eating I rest.
I have been learning German for a year.
Das ist ein Buch **von** Goethe. / Der Brief ist **von meiner** Mutter.
That is a book by Goethe. / The letter is from my mother.
Ich gehe **zu meinem** Arzt. / **Zum** Geburtstag schenke ich dir etwas.
I am going to my doctor. / For your birthday I am giving you something.
No one is here except me.
The hotel is opposite the station.

sections.title: 7.3 Two-Way Prepositions (Wechselpräpositionen)
sections.intro: Two-way prepositions use **Accusative for movement/direction** and **Dative for location/rest**. The nine are: **an, auf, hinter, in, neben, über, unter, vor, zwischen**. The key question: ask yourself *Wohin?* (where to? → Accusative) or *Wo?* (where? → Dative). Example: Ich lege das Buch **auf den** Tisch (Acc — putting it there) vs. Das Buch liegt **auf dem** Tisch (Dat — it's already there). This Akk/Dat alternation is one of the most important patterns in German.
tables: Ich hänge das Bild **an die** Wand.
Ich lege das Buch **auf den** Tisch.
Ich hänge die Lampe **über den** Tisch.
Er fährt das Auto **vor das** Haus.
Er stellt den Stuhl **zwischen die** Tische.

sections.title: 7.4 Genitive Prepositions (mainly A2)
tables: **Während des** Sommers fahre ich ans Meer.
During summer I go to the sea.

sections.title: 8.1 Coordinating Conjunctions – no word order change
sections.intro: Coordinating conjunctions sit at **Position 0** — before the main clause — and do not affect word order. The main ones: **und** (and), **aber** (but), **oder** (or), **denn** (because/for — always followed by normal order), **sondern** (but rather — used after a negative). Pattern: both clauses keep normal V2 word order. Denn vs. weil: both mean 'because' but denn is coordinating (normal order) and weil is subordinating (verb to end).
tables: Ich lerne Deutsch **und** mein Bruder lernt Spanisch.
Möchtest du Tee **oder** möchtest du Kaffee?
Er ist reich, **aber** er ist nicht glücklich.
States a reason; less common in speech
Ich bleibe zu Hause, **denn** es regnet stark.
ONLY after a negation; expresses an alternative
Er wohnt nicht in Berlin, **sondern** in Hamburg.
Er wollte kommen, **doch** er hatte keine Zeit.
Das Ergebnis war gut, **jedoch** nicht perfekt.

sections.title: 8.2 Subordinating Conjunctions – verb goes to END
sections.intro: Subordinating conjunctions introduce a **dependent clause** and send the conjugated verb to the **very end** of that clause. Common ones: **weil** (because), **dass** (that), **wenn** (when/if), **obwohl** (although), **als** (when — one-time past), **während** (while), **bevor** (before), **nachdem** (after). When the subordinate clause comes first, the main clause inverts: verb comes immediately after the comma. Pattern: [Subclause + VERB], [VERB + Subject...].
tables: Ich bleibe zu Hause, weil ich **krank bin**.
Most common reason conjunction in speech
Da es **regnet**, nehmen wir ein Taxi.
More formal than weil; often starts the sentence
After verbs of saying, thinking, hoping, knowing…
Wenn ich Zeit **habe**, rufe ich an.
Also used for repeated past events
Als ich Kind **war**, wohnte ich in Wien.
Only for one-time past events (NOT ongoing)
Putz dir die Zähne, bevor du **schläfst**.
Nachdem ich gegessen **hatte**, ging ich spazieren.
The after-clause event happened FIRST – often with Plusquamperfekt
Seitdem sie hier **wohnt**, ist sie glücklicher.
Ongoing since a point in the past
Ich lerne viel, damit ich die Prüfung **bestehe**.
Subject of main and sub clause differ; compare: um…zu
Er war müde, sodass er früh **einschlief**.

examples.de: Ich weiß, dass er früh aufstehen **muss**.
examples.en: I know that he has to get up early.

examples.de: Weil sie schlafen **will**, macht sie das Licht aus.
examples.en: Because she wants to sleep, she turns off the light.
He says that he can come.

sections.title: 8.3 Two-Part Conjunctions (Mehrteilige Konjunktionen)
sections.intro: Chapter 9

Sie ist **nicht nur** klug, **sondern auch** fleißig.
Er ist **zwar** müde, **aber** er arbeitet weiter.

sections.title: 8.4 Adverbial Connectors (Position 1) – Inversion
sections.intro: Some connectors are not coordinating conjunctions like und/aber/oder/denn — they are adverbs that link two main clauses while occupying Position 1 themselves. Because the conjugated verb must stay in Position 2 (V2 rule), the subject is pushed to Position 3. Pattern: Connector (Pos 1) -> Verb (Pos 2) -> Subject (Pos 3) -> rest. This is called inversion. Common ones: deshalb/deswegen/daher (therefore), trotzdem/dennoch (nevertheless), also (so), dann (then), sonst (otherwise), außerdem (besides), allerdings (however).
tables: Es regnet, **und ich bleibe** zu Hause.
Es regnet, **deshalb bleibe ich** zu Hause.
Es regnet, **deshalb bleibe ich** zu Hause.
It's raining, that's why I'm staying home.
Sie ist krank, **daher kann sie** nicht kommen.
She is ill, hence she can't come.
Er war müde, **trotzdem ging er** joggen.
He was tired, nevertheless he went jogging.
Es war teuer, **dennoch kaufte sie** es.
It was expensive, nevertheless she bought it.
Ich habe verschlafen, **also komme ich** später.
I overslept, so I'm coming later.
Beeil dich, **sonst verpassen wir** den Bus.
Hurry up, otherwise we'll miss the bus.
Das Hotel ist teuer, **außerdem liegt es** weit weg.
The hotel is expensive, besides it's far away.
That's true, however details are still missing.

examples.de: Ich habe keine Zeit, **deshalb gehe ich** nicht mit.
examples.en: I don't have time, that's why I'm not coming along.

examples.de: Sie spricht kein Englisch, **trotzdem hat sie** den Job bekommen.
examples.en: She doesn't speak English, nevertheless she got the job.

examples.de: Wir hatten kein Geld, **also blieben wir** zu Hause.
examples.en: We had no money, so we stayed home.

sections.title: 9.1 Complete W-Question Reference
sections.intro: German W-question words (Fragewörter) follow the verb-second (V2) rule: question word → verb → subject. The question word occupies Position 1, the verb is always in Position 2. Key distinction: **wer** (who — Nominative), **wen** (whom — Accusative), **wem** (whom — Dative), **wessen** (whose — Genitive). For things and places: **was** (what), **wo** (where — location), **wohin** (where to — direction), **woher** (where from). Time: **wann** (when), **wie lange** (how long), **wie oft** (how often).

sections.title: 9.2 Preposition + Question Word (wo-/da- compounds)
sections.intro: When asking about a **thing** or **idea** (not a person) combined with a preposition, use a **wo-** compound. The answer uses a **da-** compound.

sections.title: 9.3 Yes / No Questions
sections.intro: In German yes/no questions, the verb moves to Position 1 (before the subject): **doch** is used to contradict a NEGATIVE question or statement. It means "yes I am / yes it is" when someone expected "no": **Anna: **Entschuldigung, wie komme ich zum Bahnhof? **Passant: **Gehen Sie geradeaus, dann links. **Anna: **Ist das weit? **Passant: **Nein, nur fünf Minuten zu Fuß.

Oh yes, of course I am learning German!
Yes it is (contrary to what you say)!

sections.title: 10.1 Modal Particles (Modalpartikeln) – adding attitude
sections.intro: Modal particles (Modalpartikeln) are one of the most characteristically German features — they add emotional colour, attitude, and nuance without changing the core meaning of a sentence. They cannot be translated directly; instead, they shift tone. They are **always unstressed** in speech. Key ones to master first: **doch** (contradiction/reminder), **mal** (casual softener for requests), **ja** (shared knowledge/mild surprise), **eben/halt** (resignation — that's just how it is). Modal particles never appear at the start of a sentence.
tables: Softening a command; correcting an assumption; pointing out the obvious
Komm **doch** mit! / Das weißt du **doch**! / Das ist **doch** nicht so schwer!
Things both parties know; exclamations; strong warning
Das ist **ja** interessant! / Du weißt **ja**, dass… / Komm **ja** pünktlich!
Expressing that something cannot be changed
Das ist **eben** so. / Dann bleib ich **halt** zu Hause.
Das wird **schon** gut gehen. / Das stimmt **schon**, aber…
When you think something is true but aren't certain
Er ist **wohl** krank. / Wo ist sie **wohl**?
Introducing a slight contrast; shifting topic
**Eigentlich** wollte ich früh schlafen. / Was meinst du **eigentlich** damit?
Was ist **bloß** passiert? / Komm **bloß** nicht zu spät!
Asking whether something unexpected is true
Bist du **etwa** krank? / Das glaubst du **etwa** nicht, oder?
At all; in general; by the way (topic shift)
Das ist **überhaupt** nicht richtig. / Magst du **überhaupt** Kaffee?

sections.intro: Focus particles highlight or restrict which element in a sentence is important.

sections.title: 11.1 Cardinal Numbers 0–1,000,000
sections.intro: German numbers follow consistent rules once you know the building blocks. **Key pattern:** For 21–99, the ones digit comes FIRST, joined by **-und-** then the tens: einundzwanzig (one-and-twenty), dreiundfünfzig (three-and-fifty). Watch out for: **dreißig** (30) uses ß not z; **sechzehn/sechzig** drop the final -s; **siebzehn/siebzig** drop the final -en. Hundreds and thousands are straightforward: zweihundert, dreitausend. Millions take a plural -en: zwei Millionen.

sections.title: 11.2 Ordinal Numbers (Ordnungszahlen)
sections.intro: Ordinal numbers (first, second, third...) are formed by adding **-te** to numbers 1–19 and **-ste** to numbers 20 and above. Three key irregulars to memorise: **erst-** (not einte-), **dritt-** (not zweite-), **siebt-** (not siebente-). Like all adjectives, ordinals must decline according to case and gender — use the same adjective ending tables you already know. In dates, ordinals are always used: am **dritten** März (Dative after am).
tables: erst- (not einte-), dritt- (not zweite-), siebt- (not siebente-), acht- (not achtete-)
erste, zweite, dritte, vierte, fünfte, sechste, siebte, achte, neunte, zehnte

sections.title: 11.3 Fractions (Bruchzahlen)
sections.intro: German fractions use **-tel** (for denominators up to 19) and **-stel** (for 20+): ein Drittel, ein Viertel, ein Hundertstel. The exception is **halb** — it behaves as an adjective (eine halbe Stunde, ein halber Liter) not as a noun. **Anderthalb** (1½) is invariable — it never changes form. Fractions as nouns are neuter: das Drittel, das Viertel.
tables: halb as adjective: eine **halbe** Stunde, ein **halber** Liter
Drittel, Viertel etc. are neuter nouns (das Drittel)

examples.en: I have been waiting for half an hour.

sections.title: 11.4 Telling the Time (Uhrzeit)
sections.intro: German has two time systems running in parallel. The **24-hour official system** (used in transport, broadcasting) is straightforward: 14:30 = vierzehn Uhr dreißig. The **colloquial 12-hour system** uses halb, Viertel, and nach/vor. The trap: **halb drei does NOT mean half past three** — it means half way to three, i.e. **2:30**. Think of halb as 'half before': halb drei = half before 3:00. Viertel nach drei = 3:15, Viertel vor drei = 2:45.
tables: Wie viel Uhr ist es? / Wie spät ist es?

sections.title: 13.1 The Golden Rule — Verb in Position 2 (V2)
sections.intro: The most fundamental rule of German sentence structure: **the conjugated verb always occupies Position 2** in a main clause — no matter what comes first. Position 1 can be the subject, an adverb, a time expression, a prepositional phrase, or even an entire subordinate clause. Whatever fills Position 1, the verb immediately follows. This is called the **V2 rule** (Verbzweitstellung). When something other than the subject occupies Position 1, the subject swaps to Position 3 — this is called **inversion**. Common mistake: English speakers often put the verb third when they front an adverb — in German this is wrong. Example: *Heute* **gehe** *ich* ins Kino (NOT: Heute ich gehe ins Kino).
tables: Subclause first — verb 2nd in main clause
examples: ✅ Heute **gehe** ich ins Kino. (correct — verb in P2)
❌ Heute ich **gehe** ins Kino. (wrong — verb in P3)
✅ Gestern **hat** er Deutsch **gelernt**. (Perfekt — auxiliary in P2, participle at end)
✅ Ich **kann** morgen leider nicht **kommen**. (Modal — modal in P2, infinitive at end)

sections.title: 13.2 The Bracket / Frame (Satzklammer)
sections.intro: One of German's most distinctive features is the **Satzklammer** (sentence bracket or verbal bracket). In many tenses and with modal verbs, the verb splits into two parts that form a frame around the middle of the sentence. The **first verbal element** sits in Position 2; the **second verbal element** (infinitive, past participle, or separable prefix) goes to the **very end** of the clause. Everything else — objects, adverbs, prepositional phrases — is sandwiched inside this bracket. This is why German sentences can feel 'long' before the meaning is complete: you must wait for the end to get the full picture. Mastering the bracket is essential for all compound tenses, modal constructions, and separable verbs.
examples: Ich **muss** morgen früh **aufstehen**. (modal + separable verb at end)
Sie **hat** ihrem Bruder ein Buch **geschenkt**. (Perfekt bracket)
Er **ruft** seine Mutter jeden Abend **an**. (separable verb — prefix goes to end)
Das **wird** nicht leicht **sein**. (Futur bracket)

sections.title: 13.3 Word Order in Main Clauses (Hauptsatz)
sections.intro: Within the bracket, the order of elements follows a principle called **TeKaMoLo**: **Te**mporal (when?) → **Ka**usal (why?) → **Mo**dal (how?) → **Lo**kal (where?). This gives the preferred sequence for adverbials inside the sentence. Additionally, **pronouns come before nouns**, and **shorter before longer** elements. For objects: the **Dative object comes before the Accusative object** unless the Accusative is a pronoun (then pronoun first). These are tendencies rather than absolute rules, but following them makes your German sound natural.
tables: gestern, um 8 Uhr, seit drei Jahren
in Berlin, nach Hause, aus München
Acc pronoun jumps before Dat noun
Acc before Dat when both pronouns
examples: Ich fahre [Te: morgen früh] [Ka: wegen der Arbeit] [Mo: mit dem Zug] [Lo: nach Hamburg].
Er hat [Te: gestern] [Mo: schnell] [Lo: nach Hause] gegangen.
Ich gebe **meinem Vater** **das Geschenk**. → Ich gebe **es** **meinem Vater**. (pronoun moves forward)

sections.title: 13.4 Subordinate Clauses — Verb to the End (Nebensatz)
sections.intro: In a **subordinate clause** (Nebensatz), introduced by a subordinating conjunction (weil, dass, wenn, obwohl, als...) or a relative pronoun, the conjugated verb moves to the **very last position** of that clause. If the verb is compound (modal + infinitive, Perfekt), both parts go to the end — the auxiliary comes last of all. Separable verbs reunite: the prefix and stem stay together at the end. When the subordinate clause comes **before** the main clause, the main clause begins with its verb (inversion): the result is Verb–Verb across the comma. This 'Verb–Verb' at the comma junction is a reliable visual marker of a fronted subordinate clause.
examples: Ich lerne Deutsch, weil es interessant **ist**. (weil → verb to end)
Er weiß nicht, ob sie morgen **kommt**. (ob = whether)
**Weil** es regnet, **bleibe** ich zu Hause. (fronted → Verb-Verb at comma)
Sie hat gesagt, dass sie das Buch gelesen **hat**. (Perfekt in Nebensatz)

sections.title: 13.5 Relative Clauses (Relativsätze)
sections.intro: A relative clause describes a noun using a **relative pronoun** (der, die, das — declines like the definite article with some exceptions). The relative pronoun agrees in **gender and number** with the noun it refers to (its antecedent), but its **case** is determined by its role inside the relative clause. Like all subordinate clauses, the verb goes to the end. The relative clause is always set off by **commas**. Key difference from the definite article: Genitive forms are **dessen/deren**, and Dative plural is **denen** (not den).
tables: Der Mann, **der** dort steht, ist mein Lehrer.
Die Frau, **die** ich kenne, heißt Anna.
Das Kind, **dem** ich helfe, ist nett.
Der Mann, **dessen** Auto rot ist, wohnt hier.
examples: Das Buch, **das** ich lese, ist sehr interessant. (neuter Acc — object of lesen)
Die Stadt, **in der** ich wohne, heißt Hamburg. (prep + Dat fem.)
Der Mann, **dessen** Tochter Ärztin ist, ist Lehrer. (Genitive — whose)
Die Studenten, **denen** ich geholfen habe, waren dankbar. (Dat plural — denen not den)

sections.title: 13.6 Questions — Word Order
sections.intro: German has two types of questions, each with distinct word order. **W-questions** (information questions) begin with a question word, followed immediately by the verb, then the subject: W-word → Verb → Subject. **Yes/No questions** have no question word — the verb simply moves to Position 1: Verb → Subject → rest. To contradict a negative yes/no question, use **doch** (yes, contrary to your expectation). Indirect questions (embedded questions) behave like subordinate clauses: conjunction/question word + verb at the end — no inversion, no question mark.
examples: **Wo** wohnst du? — Ich wohne in Berlin. (W-question)
**Sprichst** du Deutsch? — Ja, ein bisschen. (Yes/No)
Kommst du nicht? — **Doch**, ich komme! (doch contradicts negative)
Kannst du mir sagen, wo der Bahnhof **ist**? (indirect question — verb last)

sections.title: 13.7 Negation — nicht vs. kein
sections.intro: German has two negation words with distinct rules. **Kein** negates **nouns** — it replaces the indefinite article (ein → kein) or stands alone before uncountable nouns: Ich habe kein Geld. Ich habe keine Zeit. **Nicht** negates everything else — verbs, adjectives, adverbs, prepositional phrases, and definite noun phrases. Position of **nicht**: general negation (of the whole verb) → nicht goes at the **end** of the middle field, just before the right bracket. Specific negation (of one element) → nicht goes directly **before** the element being negated. Nicht never comes before the subject. Key contrast: Ich habe **kein** Auto (no article → kein) vs. Ich fahre **nicht** das Auto (definite → nicht).
examples: Ich habe **kein** Geld. (indefinite → kein)
Ich habe **das** Geld **nicht**. (definite → nicht, end position)
Er ist **nicht** müde. (adjective → nicht before it)
Sie kommt heute **nicht**. (general negation → nicht at end)
Er ruft **nicht** an. (separable → nicht before prefix an)

sections.title: 13.8 Passive Voice (Passiv)
sections.intro: German has two passive constructions. The **Vorgangspassiv** (process passive) describes an action being performed — formed with **werden + past participle**: Das Buch wird gelesen (The book is being read). The **Zustandspassiv** (state passive) describes the result of an action — formed with **sein + past participle**: Das Buch ist gelesen (The book has been read / is read). The agent (doer) is expressed with **von + Dative**: Das Buch wird von der Lehrerin gelesen. The means/instrument uses **durch + Accusative**: Die Stadt wurde durch ein Erdbeben zerstört. Impersonal passive (Es wird getanzt) describes general activity with no specific subject — 'There is dancing going on'.
examples: Das Haus **wird** gebaut. (Vorgangspassiv — ongoing)
Das Haus **ist** gebaut. (Zustandspassiv — finished state)
Der Brief **wurde** von der Sekretärin **geschrieben**. (agent with von)
Das Fenster **muss** geschlossen **werden**. (modal passive)

sections.title: 13.9 Konjunktiv II — Conditional & Polite Forms
sections.intro: The **Konjunktiv II** expresses hypothetical situations, wishes, polite requests, and unreal conditions. For most verbs in everyday speech, it is formed with **würde + infinitive** (equivalent of English 'would'): Ich würde gerne kommen. However, modal verbs, sein, haben, and a handful of common verbs use their own Konjunktiv II forms (which look like Präteritum + umlaut). The most important: **wäre** (sein), **hätte** (haben), **könnte** (können), **müsste** (müssen), **dürfte** (dürfen), **sollte** (sollen), **wollte** (wollen), **möchte** (mögen). Conditional sentences have two halves: the **wenn-clause** (condition) and the **Hauptsatz** (result) — both use Konjunktiv II.
examples: **Wenn** ich Zeit **hätte**, **würde** ich mehr lesen. (unreal condition)
**Könnten** Sie mir bitte sagen, wo der Bahnhof ist? (polite request)
Ich **wäre** gerne mitgekommen. (regret about the past)
Das **wäre** schön! (That would be nice — common expression)

sections.title: 13.1 0 Common Sentence Patterns at a Glance
sections.intro: Here is a summary of the most important German sentence patterns, from simplest to most complex. Learning to recognise these patterns helps you both understand native speech and construct correct sentences. The underlying logic is always the same: **verb second in main clauses, verb last in subordinate clauses**, with the bracket holding the structure together.
tables: Wenn + Subj + Konj.II(end), würde + Subj + Inf
examples: V2 rule: no matter what is in P1, the verb is always P2.
Bracket rule: P2 verb + ... + end verb = everything in between is the 'filling'.
Subordinate clause: conjunction signals 'verb goes to end'.
Inversion: fronted element + verb + subject (subject never P1 after inversion).

sections.title: What is Auslautverhärtung?
sections.intro: **Auslautverhärtung** (literally "end-sound hardening") is one of the most important phonological rules in German. It states: **voiced consonants become voiceless when they appear at the end of a syllable or word**. The *spelling* never changes — only the *sound* does. This means a word like **Hund** is written with a 'd' but pronounced with a 't' sound. Understanding this rule is essential for both correct pronunciation and for understanding how German words are structured.
examples: **Auslaut** = the final sound of a word or syllable
**Verhärtung** = hardening (voiced → voiceless)

examples.en: written 'd' → pronounced [t] at the end: [hʊnt]
written 'g' → pronounced [k] at the end: [taːk]
written 'b' → pronounced [p] at the end: [diːp]

sections.title: Voiced vs. Voiceless Consonants
sections.intro: Consonants are either **voiced** (vocal cords vibrate) or **voiceless** (vocal cords do not vibrate). Place your fingers on your throat — you will feel a buzz for voiced sounds and nothing for voiceless. German has six pairs where Auslautverhärtung applies. Each **voiced** consonant has a **voiceless twin** that sounds the same but without the voice.
tables: Voiced (in the middle of a word)
Voiceless (at the end of a word)
examples: **Remember:** the spelling stays the same — only the pronunciation changes at the end of a word or syllable.
**Tip:** To check whether a word has a voiced or voiceless final consonant, add a vowel suffix: *Hund → Hunde* — you can hear the [d] return in *Hunde*!

sections.title: b → p: The 'b' Becomes 'p' at the End
sections.intro: Whenever a word or syllable ends in **b**, it is pronounced as **[p]**. Add a vowel ending (like the plural -e) and the [b] sound returns. This is the clearest proof that the underlying consonant is a 'b', not a 'p'.

examples.en: but: gelbe Blumen → [ˈɡɛlbə] — the [b] returns before a vowel
but: halbe Stunde → [ˈhalbə] — same underlying /b/
but: liebe Maria → [ˈliːbə] — spelling reveals the truth
but: loben (to praise) → [ˈloːbən]

sections.title: d → t: The 'd' Becomes 't' at the End
sections.intro: The **d → t** devoicing is the most commonly encountered pair because so many high-frequency German words end in **-nd, -ld, -rd, -d**. Native German speakers say these words automatically with a [t], but the underlying letter is still 'd' — confirmed by the inflected forms.

examples.en: but: Freunde (friends) → [ˈfʁɔʏ̯ndə]
but: Abende → [ˈaːbəndə]
but: Helden → [ˈhɛldən]
but: Wälder → [ˈvɛldɐ]

examples: **Watch out:** *Rat* (advice) and *Rad* (wheel) are both [raːt] — you must use context or inflection to distinguish them!

sections.title: g → k: The 'g' Becomes 'k' at the End
sections.intro: Words ending in **g** are pronounced with a **[k]** sound. This affects many common words. Note: in some southern German and Austrian varieties, final **g** is realised as [x] or [ç] instead of [k] — but Standard German (Hochdeutsch) uses [k].

examples.en: Note: '-ig' at word end is pronounced [ɪç] in Standard German, not [ɪk]!
but: heilige Nacht → [ˈhaɪ̯lɪɡə] — the [ɡ] returns before a vowel
but: hungrige Kinder → [ˈhʊŋʁɪɡə]

examples: **Special rule:** the suffix **-ig** at the very end of a word is pronounced **[ɪç]** in Standard German, not [ɪk]. But before a vowel suffix, the 'g' is restored: [ɡ].

sections.title: v → f: The 'v' at the End Sounds Like 'f'
sections.intro: German **v** is already pronounced [f] in most native German words (e.g. *Vater, Vogel*). In words of foreign origin (mostly from Latin/French/English), **v** is pronounced [v] in the middle of a word but devoiced to [f] at the end. Similarly, words ending in **-iv, -av, -ov** follow this pattern.

examples.en: The test was relatively hard. → [ʁelaˈtiːf]
She is very active. → [akˈtiːf]
The motive is clear. → [moˈtiːf]

examples: **Note:** Native German words beginning with 'v' (Vater, Vogel, vier) are *always* [f] — this is not Auslautverhärtung but simply German pronunciation of 'v'.

sections.title: s → [s]: Voiced 's' Becomes Voiceless at the End
sections.intro: In German, the letter **s** is pronounced **[z]** (voiced) when it appears at the start of a syllable before a vowel. However, at the **end** of a word or syllable, 's' is pronounced **[s]** (voiceless). The plural forms of these words often reveal the underlying [z] when a vowel follows.

examples.en: but: die Häuser → [ˈhɔʏ̯zɐ] — [z] returns before the vowel suffix
but: die Preise → [ˈpʁaɪ̯zə] — voiced [z] mid-word

examples: **Contrast:** *lesen* (to read) → [ˈleːzən]: the 's' here is between vowels so it stays voiced [z] throughout.

sections.title: Spelling Stays the Same — Only Sound Changes
sections.intro: A fundamental principle of Auslautverhärtung is that **the written form (spelling) is never affected** — only the spoken form changes. German spelling is largely **morphophonemic**: it represents the underlying form of the word, which is consistent across all inflected forms. This makes spelling more logical than pronunciation alone might suggest.
tables: What you hear at the end
examples: **Key insight:** Because spelling reflects the underlying form, you can always identify the root consonant by finding an inflected form. *Hund → Hunde* shows the root is /d/.

examples.en: Both are [raːt] alone — context and case endings tell them apart
[bʊnt] vs [bʊnt] — identical at word end! Inflect to distinguish: Bünde vs. bunte

examples: **Practical rule:** When you're unsure how to spell a word, inflect it (add -e, -er, -en) and listen to the consonant — what you hear before the vowel is what you write.

sections.title: How to Find the Root Consonant
sections.intro: Because Auslautverhärtung masks the true underlying consonant, German learners need a reliable strategy to identify it. The method is simple: **add a vowel ending** to the word. In the inflected form, the voiced consonant resurfaces. This is also why German grammar instruction teaches noun plurals, verb conjugations, and adjective declension simultaneously with base forms.
examples: **Step 1:** Hear the word at the end — you get the devoiced form.
**Step 2:** Inflect or derive a related word with a vowel suffix.
**Step 3:** Listen to the consonant before the vowel — that is the root consonant.

examples.en: Root is /d/ → spelled 'Kind', not 'Kint'
Root is /b/ → spelled 'gelb', not 'gelp'

sections.title: Auslautverhärtung in Compound Words
sections.intro: German loves compound words (Komposita). When a word with a voiced final consonant becomes the **first element** of a compound, Auslautverhärtung still applies if the syllable boundary creates a final position. However, the morpheme boundary is transparent — good spellers recognise each component.

examples.en: 'd' in Hand is devoiced even inside the compound
'g' in Zug devoiced → [k] before the second element
Tage- has -e linking element → 'g' is voiced [ɡ] again

examples: **Tip:** Linking elements like **-e-, -es-, -s-, -en-** between compound parts often restore the voiced consonant.

sections.title: Common Mistakes for English Speakers
sections.intro: English does **not** have Auslautverhärtung — English final voiced consonants stay voiced (*bed, bag, rob* all keep their voiced endings). This means English speakers must consciously practise devoicing German final consonants. Below are the most common pronunciation errors and how to fix them.
tables: No voiced stops at word end
examples: **Practice drill:** Say the inflected form first (where the consonant is voiced), then say the base form — your mouth learns to devoice automatically.

examples.de: Practice: Hunde → Hund / Tage → Tag / Diebe → Dieb
examples.en: Say the plural, then drop the -e: the final consonant should change!

examples.de: Kinder → Kind / Länder → Land / Pferde → Pferd
examples.en: Plural → singular: feel the consonant harden at the end

examples: **Memory trick:** In German, consonants at the end of a word always 'lose their voice' — they get quieter, softer, and voiceless. Think of a sound 'fading out' at the end.

sections.title: Quick Reference: All Pairs at a Glance
sections.intro: Use this table as a rapid reference. For every voiced consonant that ends a German word, the corresponding voiceless sound is used in speech — while the spelling preserves the original voiced letter.
examples: **Golden rule:** If a German word ends in b, d, or g — always pronounce it as p, t, or k.
**Test yourself:** Cover the pronunciation column and say each word aloud. Then uncover and check!

examples.en: Practice makes perfect — the more you speak, the more automatic Auslautverhärtung becomes.

sections.title: Konjunktiv II — Bildung (Formation)
sections.intro:
The **Konjunktiv II** (subjunctive II) is used to express hypothetical situations, wishes, polite requests, and unreal conditions. It is one of the most important advanced German structures.

**Formation of Konjunktiv II:**
- **Modal & irregular verbs**: use the Präteritum stem + umlaut (where possible) + Konjunktiv endings (-e, -est, -e, -en, -et, -en)
- **Regular verbs**: use **würde + Infinitive** (most common in spoken German)
- **sein, haben** and modals have their own Konjunktiv II forms used directly
examples: Wenn ich Zeit **hätte**, **würde** ich mehr reisen. (If I had time, I would travel more.)
Das **wäre** schön! (That would be nice!)
Ich **könnte** dir helfen, wenn du willst. (I could help you if you want.)
**Würdest** du mir bitte helfen? (Would you please help me?)

sections.title: Konjunktiv II — Verwendung (Usage)
sections.intro:
The Konjunktiv II is used in several important contexts at B2 level:

**1. Irreale Bedingungssätze (Unreal conditionals):** Wenn + Konjunktiv II in the *wenn*-clause, Konjunktiv II in the main clause.
**2. Irreale Wunschsätze (Unreal wishes):** Often with *wenn doch* or *wenn nur*.
**3. Höfliche Bitten (Polite requests):** *Könnten Sie…? Würden Sie…? Hätten Sie…?*
**4. Ratschläge (Advice):** *An deiner Stelle würde ich… / Du solltest…*
tables: Wenn ich Geld **hätte**, **würde** ich ein Haus kaufen.
If I had money, I would buy a house.
If only I had more time!
An deiner Stelle **würde** ich das nicht tun.
In your position I wouldn't do that.
That would be a good idea.
examples: Wenn es nicht so kalt **wäre**, **würde** ich spazieren gehen. (If it weren't so cold, I would go for a walk.)
**Hätten** Sie vielleicht einen Moment Zeit? (Would you perhaps have a moment?)
Ich **würde** das an deiner Stelle überdenken. (I would reconsider that in your position.)

sections.title: Vorgangspassiv (Action Passive)
sections.intro:
The **Vorgangspassiv** (process/action passive) describes an action happening to the subject. It focuses on the action itself, not the person performing it.

**Formation:** **werden** (conjugated) + **Partizip II**

The agent (who does it) is introduced with **von + Dativ**.
examples: Der Brief **wird** heute **geschrieben**. (The letter is being written today.)
Das Gesetz **wurde** 2020 **geändert**. (The law was changed in 2020.)
Die Stadt **ist** von Touristen **besucht worden**. (The city has been visited by tourists.)
Der Bericht **wird** vom Chef **gelesen**. (The report is read by the boss.)

sections.title: Zustandspassiv & Passiversatz (State Passive & Passive Alternatives)
sections.intro:
**Zustandspassiv** describes a state (result), not the action itself.
Formation: **sein** + **Partizip II**

**Passive alternatives** (Passiversatz) express passive meaning without *werden*:
tables: A new house is being built here.
The text still needs to be corrected.
examples: Die Tür **ist geschlossen**. (The door is closed.) [Zustandspassiv]
Das **lässt sich** nicht beweisen. (That cannot be proven.)
Der Bericht **ist** bis Freitag **abzugeben**. (The report must be submitted by Friday.)

sections.title: Kausale & konzessive Nebensätze
sections.intro:
**Kausale Nebensätze** (causal clauses) give reasons. **Konzessive Nebensätze** (concessive clauses) express a contrast or concession.

Key conjunctions:
tables: Ich bleibe zu Hause, **weil** ich krank bin.
I stay home because I am ill.
**Da** es regnet, nehme ich einen Schirm.
Since it is raining, I take an umbrella.
Er geht aus, **obwohl** er müde ist.
He goes out although he is tired.
**Obgleich** sie Hunger hat, isst sie nichts.
Although she is hungry, she eats nothing.
**Auch wenn** es schwer ist, gebe ich nicht auf.
Even if it is hard, I do not give up.
examples: Sie hat den Job bekommen, **obwohl** sie wenig Erfahrung hatte. (She got the job although she had little experience.)
**Da** ich früh aufstehen muss, gehe ich jetzt schlafen. (Since I have to get up early, I am going to sleep now.)

sections.title: Finale, konsekutive & modale Nebensätze
sections.intro: **Finalsätze** express purpose (why). **Konsekutivsätze** express consequences. **Modalsätze** express how.
tables: Er lernt, **damit** er die Prüfung besteht.
He studies so that he passes the exam.
She saves (in order) to buy a house.
Es regnet stark, **so dass** die Straße überflutet ist.
It rains heavily so that the road is flooded.
Er war so müde, **sodass** er sofort einschlief.
He was so tired that he fell asleep immediately.
Sie hat geholfen, **ohne dass** jemand sie fragte.
She helped without anyone asking her.
examples: Ich stehe früh auf, **um** den Sonnenaufgang **zu** sehen. (I get up early in order to see the sunrise.)
Er spricht sehr leise, **so dass** ich ihn kaum verstehe. (He speaks very quietly so that I can barely understand him.)
Sie hat das Problem gelöst, **indem** sie kreativ gedacht hat. (She solved the problem by thinking creatively.)

sections.title: Relativsätze mit Präpositionen & Relativpronomen wo-
sections.intro: At B2, relative clauses become more complex with **prepositions + relative pronoun** or **wo- compounds** for non-personal referents.
tables: Das ist das Thema, **über das** wir sprechen.
That is the topic we are talking about.
Der Mann, **mit dem** sie arbeitet, ist nett.
The man she works with is nice.
That is something I think about.
Das Tool, **womit** wir arbeiten, ist neu.
The tool we work with is new.
examples: Das ist das Buch, **von dem** ich dir erzählt habe. (That is the book I told you about.)
Es gibt viel, **worüber** wir reden müssen. (There is a lot we need to talk about.)
Nichts, **was** sie tat, war falsch. (Nothing she did was wrong.)

sections.title: Partizip I & II als Adjektive und in Konstruktionen
sections.intro: **Partizip I** (present participle = Verb + -end) describes an ongoing action. **Partizip II** (past participle) describes a completed or passive action. Both can be used as adjectives (with adjective endings) or in reduced relative clauses, replacing longer subordinate clauses.
tables: the position that is being offered
examples: Die **steigende** Inflation ist ein Problem. (Rising inflation is a problem.)
Der **vorgeschlagene** Plan klingt gut. (The proposed plan sounds good.)
Das **gewonnene** Spiel war spannend. (The won game was exciting.)

sections.title: Infinitivkonstruktionen (Infinitive Constructions)
sections.intro: Extended infinitive constructions with **zu + Infinitiv** can replace subordinate clauses, making sentences more concise and formal. Many common verbs are followed directly by **zu + Infinitiv** when the subject of both clauses is the same: versuchen, hoffen, planen, beginnen, vergessen, vorhaben, aufhören, sich freuen (auf/über).
tables: He studies in order to be successful.
I have a lot to do.
That still needs to be done.
He tries to arrive on time.
I hope to see you soon.
We plan to travel to Berlin.
She is starting to learn German.
He often forgets to lock the door.
I'm looking forward to seeing you.
examples: Sie fährt nach Berlin, **um** ihre Familie **zu besuchen**. (She goes to Berlin to visit her family.)
Er hat aufgehört, **ohne** sich **zu verabschieden**. (He left without saying goodbye.)
**Anstatt** das Problem **zu ignorieren**, sucht sie eine Lösung. (Instead of ignoring the problem, she looks for a solution.)

examples.en: He tries to do sports every day.
I hope to pass the exam.

sections.title: Zweiteilige Konnektoren (Two-Part Connectors)
sections.intro: Two-part connectors link clauses and emphasise contrast, addition, or alternatives. They are essential for well-structured arguments at B2 level.
tables: **Sowohl** der Preis **als auch** die Qualität sind wichtig.
**Entweder** kommst du mit, **oder** ich gehe allein.
**Nicht nur** der Inhalt, **sondern auch** die Form zählt.
Das ist **zwar** teuer, **aber** es lohnt sich.
**Je** mehr man lernt, **desto/umso** besser versteht man.
examples: **Sowohl** die Regierung **als auch** die Bevölkerung sind gefordert. (Both the government and the population are required.)
**Je** früher man beginnt, **desto** leichter fällt das Lernen. (The earlier you start, the easier learning becomes.)
Das war **zwar** schwierig, **aber** wir haben es geschafft. (That was admittedly difficult, but we managed it.)
**Je** länger die Prüfung dauert, **umso** müder werde ich. (The longer the exam lasts, the more tired I become.)

sections.title: Modalpartikeln & Abtönungspartikeln (Modal Particles)
sections.intro: Modal particles are small words that add nuance, tone, and attitude to sentences. They are unstressed and cannot be translated literally — but they are essential for natural German at B2+.
examples: Das hast du **doch** selbst gesagt! (But you said that yourself!)
Er kommt **wohl** nicht mehr. (He probably won't come anymore.)
Das ist **eben** das Problem. (That is simply the problem.)
Schau **mal**, was ich gefunden habe! (Look what I found!)

sections.title: Nominalstil & Funktionsverbgefüge (Nominal Style)
sections.intro: Formal German (newspapers, official documents, academic writing) uses **Nominalstil** — turning verbs into nouns + function verbs. Recognising these structures is essential at B2.
examples: Die Regierung hat **eine Entscheidung getroffen**. (The government made a decision.)
Er hat **einen wichtigen Beitrag geleistet**. (He made an important contribution.)
Bitte **stellen Sie Ihre Fragen** am Ende. (Please ask your questions at the end.)

sections.title: Indirekte Rede (Reported Speech)
sections.intro: **Indirekte Rede** (reported/indirect speech) is used to report what someone said without quoting them directly. In formal German (journalism, official reports), **Konjunktiv I** is used; in everyday speech, Konjunktiv II or *würde* constructions are common.
examples: Der Minister erklärte, die Lage **sei** unter Kontrolle. (The minister stated that the situation was under control.)
Die Sprecherin teilte mit, das Unternehmen **habe** Gewinne erzielt. (The spokesperson announced that the company had made profits.)
Er behauptete, er **könne** das Problem lösen. (He claimed he could solve the problem.)
